steps: 1. Recall the fundamental property of Euclidean plane geometry: the sum of the interior angles of any triangle is always 180°. 2. This can be derived from the fact that a straight line is 180°, and by drawing a parallel line to one side through the opposite vertex, alternate interior angles show the three angles of the triangle combine to form a straight line. 3. Check the options: A) 90° is too small (right triangle alone has 90° plus two acute angles). B) 180° matches the known theorem. C) 270° is the sum of three right angles, not possible in a triangle. D) 360° is the sum of angles in a quadrilateral. E) 540° is the sum of interior angles of a pentagon. 4. Therefore, the correct answer is 180°.
